entry.title: Clarification on Using BW DataSources in Datasphere Migration
entry.content:
Hello SAP Team,

We are currently migrating our BW data flows to SAP Datasphere.

During this migration, we have encountered a challenge in identifying appropriate CDS views for certain objects. Some of the required fields are custom fields that were previously used in BW DSOs, and for these cases we are currently relying on BW DataSources (For Example: 0HR_PA_0, 0HR_PA_1, 0HRPOSITION_ATTR and 0EC_PCA_1). 

Can we use these Datasources or do we need to create CDS views ? Which one is preferable ?

However, we are unable to identify equivalent CDS views or the corresponding underlying tables for some of these fields.

Could you please confirm whether using BW DataSources in such scenarios is considered a recommended or acceptable best practice when equivalent CDS views are not available?

Additionally, if it is acceptable to use BW DataSources in these cases, could you also confirm whether the same approach can be applied consistently across other functional modules where similar limitations exist?

We would appreciate your guidance on this matter.

Thank you in advance for your support.

 

Regards,
Radhakrushna

entry.title: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 2 — Roll & Move
entry.content:
Week 2 — Roll & Move

Challenge series: Snake & Ladder Developer Challenge | Week 2 of 4 Dates: 13–19 July 2026

The Setup

You have a board. You have a player state machine. This week we will make it move.

This week you implement the bound action handlers that power the game: dice rolling, position calculation, and snake/ladder logic. CAP's @flow.status machinery will automatically enforce that moves only happen in the right state — your job is to write the business logic inside each handler.

By the end of this week you can play a full single-player game from the command line using plain OData calls.

What You'll Build This Week

  • Handler implementations for rollDice, confirmMove, blockPlayer, unblockPlayer, and winGame
  • Snake and ladder position resolution (including double-headed snake logic)
  • HTTP 409 proof: demonstrating that calling an action in the wrong state is rejected by CAP
  • An end-to-end single-player run reaching square 100

How CAP Enforces State Transitions

Before your handler code runs, CAP checks the current TurnStatus of the player entity against the @from annotation on the action. If the status doesn't match, CAP returns:

HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
{
  "error": {
    "code": "409",
    "message": "Action 'rollDice' is not allowed in current status 'Waiting'"
  }
}

No code from you. No if (status !== 'Playing') throw .... The annotation does it.

After your handler returns (without throwing), CAP updates the status field to the @TO value. Again — no code from you.

This is the key insight of the week: your handler only contains business logic, not state management.

Step-by-Step

1. Create the service implementation file

Create srv/game-service.js:

const cds = require('@sap/cds')

module.exports = class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  async init() {

    const { Players, BoardSquares } = this.entities

    // ── startTurn ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Waiting]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @to: #Playing      →  CAP updates status after this returns
    // No business logic needed — CAP's @flow.status handles the transition.
    this.on('startTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Intentionally empty: the @from/@to annotations do all the work.
    })

    // ── rollDice ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Playing]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @to: #Moving       →  CAP updates status after this returns
    this.on('rollDice', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      const roll = Math.ceil(Math.random() * 6)
      let newPosition = player.position + roll

      // Can't overshoot square 100 — must land exactly
      if (newPosition > 100) {
        await UPDATE(Players).set({ lastRoll: roll }).where({ ID: player.ID })
        const needed = 100 - player.position
        return req.reject(409, `Need exactly ${needed} to finish. Rolled ${roll}. No move.`)
      }

      // Resolve snake or ladder on the new square
      const square = await SELECT.one.from(BoardSquares).where({ square: newPosition })
      let finalPosition = newPosition
      let event = null

      if (square?.ladderTo) {
        finalPosition = square.ladderTo
        event = { type: 'ladder', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      } else if (square?.snakeTo) {
        finalPosition = square.snakeTo
        event = square.isDoubleHead
          ? { type: 'doubleSnake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
          : { type: 'snake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      }

      // Persist position and last roll
      await UPDATE(Players)
        .set({ position: finalPosition, lastRoll: roll })
        .where({ ID: player.ID })

      // Emit domain event so subscribers (Week 3) can react
      if (event) await this.emit('BoardEvent', { playerID: player.ID, ...event })

      // Use req.reply() — not return — to pin the response value before CAP's
      // @flow.status after-handler runs. A plain `return` gets cleared by the
      // flow machinery, producing HTTP 204 instead of the expected 200 + body.
      req.reply({ roll, position: finalPosition, event: event?.type ?? 'normal' })
    })

    // ── confirmMove ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  normal end of a turn, hands off to next player (Week 3)
    // @to: #Waiting     →  player waits for their next turn
    this.on('confirmMove', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Week 3 will add turn rotation here.
      // For now: just let CAP apply @to: #Waiting
    })

    // ── blockPlayer ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player lands on a double-headed snake
    // @to: #Blocked     →  player skips their next turn
    this.on('blockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Business logic: the position was already updated in rollDice.
      // This action just transitions state — CAP handles the @to: #Blocked update.
      req.info('Player is blocked and will skip their next turn.')
    })

    // ── unblockPlayer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
    // @to: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
    this.on('unblockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // CAP's $flow.previous does the work — no code needed here.
      // The player's status returns to #Waiting automatically.
    })

    // ── winGame ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player.position === 100
    // @to: #Finished    →  player has won
    this.on('winGame', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      // Mark the session as finished with this player as winner
      await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
        .set({
          finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
          winner_ID: player.ID
        })
        .where({ ID: player.session_ID })

      await this.emit('GameWon', { playerID: player.ID, sessionID: player.session_ID })
    })

    return super.init()
  }
}

If you notice rollDice action response looks different from how it was defined last week.

Lets adapt it as shown in srv/game-service.cds

  @from: [ #Playing ]
  @to: #Moving
  action rollDice() returns { roll: Integer; position: Integer; event: String; };

 

2. Add the domain events to your service CDS

Add these to the bottom of srv/game-service.cds:

// Domain events — emitted by handlers, consumed in Week 3
event BoardEvent {
  playerID : UUID;
  type     : String;   // 'ladder' | 'snake' | 'doubleSnake'
  from  : Integer;  // 'from' is a CDS reserved word — escape with ![]
  ![to]    : Integer;
}

event GameWon {
  playerID  : UUID;
  sessionID : UUID;
}

An error is intentionally introduced in the snippet above with the hint.  

3. Test the state machine manually

Start the server:

cds watch

First, create a player using the REST API or the Fiori Launchpad at http://localhost:4004. Then test the status flow:

Note on service path: CAP derives the OData path from the service name. GameService maps to /odata/v4/game (lowercase, stripped of the "Service" suffix). All example requests below use this path. You can confirm the mounted path in the cds watch console output: serving GameService { at: '/odata/v4/game' }.

Step 1 — Start a turn (transitions Waiting → Playing):

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)/startTurn
Content-Type: application/json
{}

Expected response: HTTP 204 No Content (empty body). This is correct — startTurn has no return type, so CAP applies the @TO: #Playing transition silently. Verify the state changed with a GET:

GET http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/GameService/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)?$select=TurnStatus

You should see "TurnStatus": "Playing".

Step 2 — Roll the dice (transitions Playing → Moving):

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)/rollDice
Content-Type: application/json
{}

Expected response: HTTP 200 with the dice roll, new position, and board event:

{
  "@context": "$metadata#GameService.rollDiceType",
  "roll": 4,
  "position": 38,
  "event": "ladder"
}

The event field is "normal", "ladder", "snake", or "doubleSnake". The position is the final square after any snake or ladder has been applied — so you can read the outcome directly from the action response without a follow-up GET.

Why req.reply() not return? CAP's @flow.status machinery runs an after-handler that updates the status field. If you use a plain return, that after-handler clears the response value and the client gets HTTP 204 No Content instead. Calling req.reply({...}) pins the response before the flow handler runs, so the body reaches the client.

Step 3 — Prove the 409 rejection:

Without calling confirmMove first, try to call rollDice again. CAP will return:

HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
{
  "error": {
    "code": "409",
    "message": "Action 'rollDice' is not allowed in current status 'Moving'"
  }
}

This is the framework working exactly as designed. Screenshot this — it's your Week 2 submission evidence.

Step 4 — Confirm the move (transitions Moving → Waiting):

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)/confirmMove
Content-Type: application/json
{}

Expected response: HTTP 204 No Content (empty body). confirmMove has no return type — the empty response is correct. Verify with a GET that TurnStatus is now "Waiting".

4. Test the double-headed snake

Manually set a player to position 88 (one roll away from square 89, a double-headed snake head):

PATCH http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/GameService/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)
Content-Type: application/json
{ "position": 88 }

Note: position is @readonly in the service — for testing, update it directly via the db entity or temporarily remove @readonly.

Then start a turn and roll — if you roll 1, you land on 89 (a double-headed snake head). Your position should move to 50 (the shared tail). The BoardEvent event should fire with type: 'doubleSnake'.

Follow up by calling blockPlayer to transition to #Blocked, then unblockPlayer to see $flow.previous restore the state.

The $flow.previous Moment

When you call unblockPlayer and see the status return to its previous value automatically — that's CAP's state history at work. No code, no stored "previousStatus" field. The framework tracks it.

Note: $flow.previous restores whatever status the entity held before the transition to #Blocked. In this flow, blockPlayer is called from #Moving, so unblockPlayer restores #Moving — not #Waiting. The game loop then calls confirmMove to finish the turn. If you call blockPlayer from a different status in future extensions, $flow.previous will restore that one instead.

Stretch Goal

Add a skipTurn action that can be called when a player is #Blocked, as an alternative to unblockPlayer, that uses @TO: $flow.previous as well but also increments a turnsBlocked counter on the player:

@from: [ #Blocked ]
@to: $flow.previous
action skipTurn();
this.on('skipTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
  const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
  await UPDATE(Players)
    .set({ turnsBlocked: (player.turnsBlocked || 0) + 1 })
    .where({ ID: player.ID })
})

How to Submit

Reply to the Week 2 blog post on SAP Community with a comment containing:

  1. Your srv/game-service.js handler implementations (paste as a JS code block)
  2. A screenshot showing the HTTP 409 response when calling rollDice in the wrong state
  3. A screenshot or OData response showing your player's position after landing on a snake or ladder
  4. One sentence: what happens if you call winGame on a player whose position is not 100? How does your implementation handle it?

What's Next

In Week 3 — Multiplayer & Events, you add a second player, implement turn rotation, and wire up a CAP event subscriber that automatically advances the game state when a move is confirmed.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
schema.cds

namespace snakeladder;

// ── Status enum — the heart of the state machine ──────────────────────────
type TurnStatus : String enum {
  Waiting;    // Session created, not yet started
  Playing;    // It's this player's turn — roll the dice!
  Moving;     // Dice rolled, player token is being moved
  Blocked;    // Player hit a double-headed snake — skips next turn
  Finished;   // This player has reached square 100 and won
}

// ── Board square — pre-loaded with snake/ladder data ──────────────────────
entity BoardSquares {
  key square      : Integer;        // 1–100
      snakeTo     : Integer;        // 0 if no snake on this square
      ladderTo    : Integer;        // 0 if no ladder on this square
      isDoubleHead: Boolean default false;  // true if this is a double-headed snake mouth
}

// ── Game session ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
entity GameSessions {
  key ID          : UUID;
      name        : String(100);    // e.g. "Friday Afternoon Game"
      createdAt   : Timestamp;
      startedAt   : Timestamp;
      finishedAt  : Timestamp;
      winner      : Association to Players;
}

// ── Player — one row per player per session ───────────────────────────────
@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
  key ID          : UUID;
      session     : Association to GameSessions;
      name        : String(100);
      @readonly
      position    : Integer default 1;      // current square (1–100)
      @readonly
      TurnStatus  : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
      turnOrder   : Integer;                // 1 = goes first
      lastRoll    : Integer;               // last dice result
}

game-service.cds

using { snakeladder } from '../db/schema';

service GameService {

  entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

  @flow.status: TurnStatus
  entity Players as projection on snakeladder.Players actions {

    @from: [ #Waiting ]
    @TO: #Playing
    action startTurn();

    @from: [ #Playing ]
    @TO: #Moving
    action rollDice() returns Integer;

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Waiting                  
    action confirmMove();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Blocked                  // double-headed snake bite
    action blockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Blocked ]
    @TO: $flow.previous            // restores pre-block status
    action unblockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Finished
    action winGame();
  }

  entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

 

 

Kept TurnStatus as it is.

entry.title: Re: Can't access Hands-on Practice for Implementing SAP S...
entry.content:
Hi Claudine,

Short answer: This is not a fault in the practice system — it is an SAP account/login (Universal ID) routing problem. Your sign-in is being redirected to SAP's internal Microsoft Entra tenant ("SAP SE"), where an external learner account cannot authenticate. It normally needs an SAP-side correction, so the fix is to sign in with the correct email-based account and, if that fails, raise a Practice Systems support case.

What the error actually means

It is a Microsoft Entra ID error, not an S/4HANA Cloud error. accounts.sap.com/msec is an application registered in SAP's own corporate Entra tenant ("SAP SE"), and your login is being routed there — but your learner account is not a member of that internal tenant, hence "does not exist in tenant / add as external user first." SAP documents this exact symptom for Universal ID logins that get wrongly redirected to Azure AD. See, for example: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/2121093/how-to-resolve-selected-user-account-does-not-exis

The current login model (context)

Since 20 January 2026, SAP permanently deactivated the old "Sign in with Universal ID" SSO. You must now log in on the SAP sign-in screen with the email address (or S-/P-user ID) plus password connected to your account; the UID is only a wallet now. Likely (inference): re-enrolling on Monday created a fresh SSO binding that is now following the wrong path / resolving your email to an SAP-internal identity — which lands you on the "SAP SE" tenant error. Reference: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3696636

What to try first

You have already cleared your cache and used incognito, so focus on the account, not the browser. On the SAP sign-in screen, choose "Use another account" and type the exact email registered to your SAP Learning account. Do not let the browser or OS auto-select a corporate "Sign in with Microsoft" work account — that auto-selection is what triggers the redirect into the SAP SE tenant.

The reliable fix (support case)

Because the same error hits your colleagues, this is almost certainly a server-side account/routing issue SAP must correct centrally — it is not fully self-fixable. Open a case from the SAP Learning help center with Service Category = "Practice Systems", and include the affected email addresses, user IDs, the course name, and a screenshot of the error. Link: https://learning.sap.com/helpcenter

Summary: The practice system is fine — your login is being misrouted to SAP's internal "SAP SE" Entra tenant. Sign in with the exact email-based SAP account (never a corporate "Sign in with Microsoft" account); and since colleagues are affected too, raise a Practice Systems support case with emails, user IDs, course name and the error screenshot so SAP can correct the routing.

Hope it helps,

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Environmental Details tab missing in Manage Locations — how is it enabled?
entry.content:
I'm trying to assign a Location Classifier to an EHS Location in Environment Management. Per the standard flow, the classifier is maintained in Manage Locations (F3404) → Environmental Details tab → Location Classifiers.

My problem: the Environmental Details tab does not appear on the location. I only see Basic Information / Persons Responsible / Address / Documents. So there's nowhere to add the classifier.

Environment: SAP S/4HANA 2025 (Fully-Activated Appliance / demo tenant).

What I've already checked:

  • The location is Active.
  • A Person Responsible with the Environmental Manager role is assigned on the location.
  • In Customizing, Specify Activities for Persons Responsible in Environment Management, the Manage activity is ticked for the Environmental Manager (person-responsible) role.
  • My user is assigned a copy of SAP_EHSM_HSS_ENVMGR — the role the IMG documentation for "Toggle Visibility of Environmental Details in Locations" names as the one the tab is delivered for. (I also have a copy of SAP_BR_ENVIRONMENTAL_MANAGER, which is what gives me the Manage Locations app.)
  • SU53 shows no authorization failure after the tab fails to appear.

Where I'm stuck: The "Toggle Visibility…" documentation says to open "Specify Roles for Environment Management", add the user role, and select its Manage checkbox. But I can't find any such user-role activity — the only related Customizing activity (Specify Activities for Persons Responsible…) only holds person-responsible role types, not SU01/business roles. So there seems to be no place to register my (copied) user role.

Questions:

  1. What actually controls the visibility of the Environmental Details tab in Manage Locations at runtime?
  2. Since standard SAP roles can't be assigned directly and we work with copies, how is a copy of SAP_EHSM_HSS_ENVMGR meant to be enabled for this tab? Is the tab tied to a specific business catalog / authorization that a role copy might drop (which would explain SU53 being clean)?
  3. Is the "Specify Roles for Environment Management" activity referenced in the documentation actually delivered, or has it been replaced by Specify Activities for Persons Responsible?

Any pointer to the exact authorization object, business catalog, or config that governs this tab would be much appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Project HomeControl 2 · Part 2 — A UI5 frontend, built from non-UI5 Web Components
entry.content:
Impressive, very cool project!

Just a small note: You can actually consume arbitrary web components in UI5 without the need of a custom wrapper UI5 control. If your web component includes a custom-elements.json, the ui5-tooling-modules package can read it and do the wrapping for you. Check out this blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/frontend-ui5-sap-fiori-blog-posts/consuming-your-own-or-external-web-components-in-ui5-applications/ba-p/14281839

Hope to see you at UI5con and chat about this. I will be there and giving a talk about this topic 🙂

entry.title: How to Link Two Analytical Queries in Embedded SAC App? "A linked dimension" Error
entry.content:
How to Link Two Analytical Queries in Embedded SAC App?
"A linked dimension for this model is not included in the visualization's dimension bindings" Error

I am creating an Embedded SAC Application in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and trying to link two Analytical Queries using Company Code. Both Company Code fields have the same data type, semantics, and values, and the link is created successfully.

However, the widget shows the error:

"A linked dimension for this model is not included in the visualization's dimension bindings."

How can I correctly connect/link these two queries? Is there any additional configuration required for linked dimensions or dimension bindings?

entry.title: Practical SAP LeanIX Modeling Guide
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

Its been a while since I published my last blog in SAP forum. That got quite some attention from the community and helped a lot of them.

Since my last blog post, I have taken up multiple roles, and I am now currently in the Enterprise Architect (EA) role. One of the primary responsibilities of an EA is to document and create artifacts in LeanIX so that it provides Leadership with a holistic picture of their Enterprise.

But sometimes, it gets difficult for Architects to build a good story/dashboard for the customers/clients in LeanIX when there is limited information available. There can be multiple reasons for that – client is too busy, no proper documentation available, LeanIX is considered just another modeling tool, value of LeanIX is not fully understood, too many uncertainty in the attributes data, etc.

I faced this challenge recently. So, it got me thinking on how to tackle this problem.  It was a tricky one because I must build everything from little/nothing while ensuring that the information presented is meaningful and the customer can relate to it.

The first step was to go back to the basics. As Elon Musk says, I go for ‘The First Principles’ method.

First principles thinking is a powerful problem-solving method that strips away assumptions and analogies to reveal fundamental truths. By breaking complex ideas into their most basic, undeniable components, you can reconstruct them in innovative ways rather than just copying what already exists.

The core source of Truth for LeanIX is its LeanIX Documentation. I went in there and put this MetaModel picture in front of me:

I mentally divided this MetaModel into two categories to help me divide-and-conquer this large landscape:

Technical Areas

Business Areas

Application

Objective

IT Component

Initiative

Tech Category

Organization

Provider

Business Capability

Data Object

Business Context

Interface

Platform

The sequence I followed is not arbitrary. Each fact sheet unlocks the next. You cannot meaningfully create a Business Capability without knowing which Applications exist, because the Applications tell you what capabilities the business has. You cannot create a Business Context without Business Capabilities, because Contexts are the end-to-end processes that use those capabilities. You cannot create an Interface without Applications and Data Objects, because an Interface is simply a named data flow between two Applications. The sequence is a dependency chain. Build the leaves before the branches, and the branches before the trunk.

 

Step

Fact Sheet

Depends On

1

Objective

Nothing — start here from public documents

2

System

Contract / subscription list

3

Application

System 

4

IT Component

Application, System

5

Tech Category

IT Component

6

Provider

IT Component, Tech Category 

7

Platform

IT Component, Provider

8

Organization

Public website / org chart research

9

Business Capability

Application, Organization, Reference Catalog

10

Business Context

Business Capability, Application, Organization

11

Initiative

Objective, Application, BC, ITC

12

Data Object

Application, Initiative 

13

Interface

Application, Data Object, IT Component 

14

Contract

Application (link back) 

Rule of thumb: If you find yourself unable to populate the relations for a fact sheet, you have probably created it too early in the sequence. Go back and build its dependencies first.

Objective Factsheet:

Once I had this MetaModel diagram in place, I knew my job was to build a Business Strategy Map for the client. But I needed some documentation from them, like, Mission, Vision, and Values statements, a SWOT Analysis, a Strategic Plan, a Balanced Scorecard (Objectives and KPIs), and Departmental Budgets. I had none of it. So, I started digging into their official website. That’s where a company publishes all its documents. After skimming through the website and various reports, I found most of the needed information in their Annual Report and Quarterly Report. I pulled out the required information from those Reports and created Objective factsheets, populated required attributes, created tags for it and went in to Reports section to ensure its in place.

 

Note: You might have to go back and forth multiple times between your factsheets and Report, and in certain cases, make some assumptions to ensure everything is in place.
I have only shown here a part of the Report as I had to skip the sensitive information of the client. If you are interested in learning how to create the Business Strategy map in a different way, you can read this Blog.

I was quite happy with the Report I generated and verified the various Objective factsheets I created. I then sat and started thinking about what I should do next? I still had no information from the client. One day an idea struck me. I had the contract from the customer, the subscriptions they had and from there I knew what systems they have in their landscape. So, I extracted the Systems details from the contract and subscriptions.

 System Factsheet:

Once I had the all the documents extracted, I filtered out the System details. I initially created one System factsheet, exported it as an excel and then started filling in the details in that excel sheet.

It’s good if you can fill in as many details as possible. I populated the following fields in Systems factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description
  • Sub-Type
  • SAP Details (if an SAP system)

 Once I had the excel sheet updated, I uploaded the excel sheet into the LeanIX tenant and everything was in place. I was quite happy with it. Since I have now created the System factsheet, I know the Application that was present in that System and the IT Component it uses. I can easily identify the Application from the extract I took earlier. And took help with the suggestive AI-capabilities of LeanIX to identify the standard IT Components.

 Application Factsheet:

I started creating the Application factsheet after the System factsheet was in place. I populated the following fields in Application factsheets and linked them to System factsheets.

  • Name
  • Description
  • Sub-Type
  • Subscription
  • Lifecycle Dates
  • 6R Classification
  • Business Criticality
  • Functional Fit
  • Technical Fit
  • Hosting Type
  • Support Type
  • Total Annual Cost

 IT Component Factsheet: 

I now have Application and System factsheets in place. So, I safely went ahead and created IT Component factsheets and linked them to Application and System factsheets.  

I populated the following fields in IT Component factsheets:

  • Name
  • Subtype
  • Description
  • Lifecycle
  • Technology Assessment
  • Tags: Baseline/ Target

I used a two-value tag group — Baseline and Target — on all IT Component fact sheets. This is one of the most powerful low-effort modeling decisions you can make early in the project.

  • Baseline tag = the current, legacy IT Components that the organisation is running today. In my case, these were the on-premise SAP components (SAP NetWeaver, SAP HANA platform, SAP BusinessObjects, BW/4HANA, etc.) that are scheduled to be decommissioned as part of the cloud   transformation.
  • Target tag = the incoming cloud components that will replace them (SAP BTP services, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors SaaS hosting, Integration Suite, etc.).

Once these tags are applied, the IT Component Roadmap report in LeanIX will automatically show you the transition — Baseline components fading out on the left, Target components ramping up on the right — without any additional configuration. This single tag distinction drives the "before and after" story for the entire transformation roadmap.

I moved on to Tech Category and Provider Factsheets now that I have IT Components in place.

 Tech Category Factsheet:

I updated the following fields in Tech Category factsheet and linked it to IT Component factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description

 Provider Factsheet:

I have the IT Component and Tech category in place, so it gets easier to create Provider Factsheet and link it to the IT Component factsheet. I added the following fields in Provider factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description

I then moved on to the Business Area of the landscape and started creating a hierarchical structure of the organization starting with the Organization Name at the top of the hierarchy.

Platform Factsheet:

Now that I had IT Components, Tech Categories, and Providers in place, it was the right time to create Platform fact sheets. Platforms represent the hosting topology — where IT Components physically or virtually run. In my case, I created four Platforms:

  • On-Premise — for legacy SAP NetWeaver, S/4HANA on-premise, and BO components
  • SAP BTP — for all SAP cloud-native and integration components
  • SAP SaaS — for SAP-hosted SaaS applications (SuccessFactors, Ariba, Concur, etc.) M
  • Microsoft SaaS — for Microsoft-hosted components (Fabric, Power BI)

 The key modeling decision: Platform links to IT Components, not to Applications directly. An Application inherits its hosting context through its IT Components. So create Platforms only after IT Components are in place, and link them at the ITC level. I created couple of Platform factsheets and updated the following attributes in them:

  • Name
  • Description

Once I had these fields filled in, I linked the factsheet to various IT Component factsheets.

Organization Factsheet:

I still did not have any information from my client about the Organization. So, I started investigating about a typical Org structure in that industry in which my client operated. Some of the information I could infer from the client’s official website. From there, bit-by-bit I started building the Organizational factsheets and populated the following fields:

  • Name
  • Subtype
  • Description
  • Location
  • Parent (if any)

I then linked the Organization Factsheet to various Application factsheets.  

Business Capability Factsheet:

Since I had the Application factsheet in place along with Organization factsheet, it was a good time to start taking a shot at the Business Capabilities. I knew there was a Reference Catalog offered by LeanIX. I also knew the industry in which my client operated. So, based features an Application provided, I started pulling in L1/L2 Business Capabilities from Reference catalog of LeanIX:

  • Name
  • Description
  • Current Maturity
  • Target Maturity
  • Parent (if any)

Once I had all fields in place, I linked it to all Application factsheets.

 Business Context Factsheet:

Now that I already had Business Capabilities in place, I started taking educated guesses for end-to-end Business Context at a high level. This was the most challenging part for me in all the factsheets. It was not easy to identify various processes that a Business Capability and Application supported. It took me some time to identify some of the processes that were supported and list them out in Business Context factsheet. I then populated the following fields in the factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description

Once I had these in place, I linked it to various Business Capability and Application factsheets.

Here’s a Practical example on how I approached identifying a Business Context:

Step 1 — Start with industry-standard process patterns

Every industry has well-known end-to-end process names. For example, a mining company, the standard processes are immediately recognisable:

Business Context

What it covers

Record to Report

Journal entry → financial close → external reporting          

Source to Pay

Sourcing → purchase order → supplier payment

Recruit to Retire

Talent acquisition → HR → payroll → offboarding

Plan to Produce

Production planning → ore extraction → copper output reporting

Acquire to Decommission

Asset purchase → maintenance → decommission

These are SAP-aligned process patterns (SAP itself uses the same names in its Solution Map). If your client runs SAP, these process names will resonate immediately with both the client's business users and IT team. For non-SAP clients, equivalents exist in APQC, TOGAF, or industry reference architectures.

Tip: Search your client's Annual Report or industry analyst reports for process language. Companies almost always describe their operations using these exact terms in investor presentations — e.g., "procure-to-pay cycle," "hire-to-retire," "order-to-cash."

  ---

Step 2 — Use your Applications as a reverse-lookup

  Each Application you already modelled tells you which process it belongs to. Work backwards:

Application in LeanIX tenant

Business Context it belongs to

SAP SuccessFactors HCM Core, SF ECP, SF WFA

Recruit to Retire

SAP Ariba Procurement, SAP Ariba Sourcing, SAP Business Network

Source to Pay

SAP S/4HANA, SAP BPC

Record to Report

SAP Concur Travel and Expense

Travel and Expense Management

SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity

Treasury and Cash Management

If an Application is hard to place, read its description or look at the Business Capabilities it's linked to — those BCs will point you to the right process family.

  ---

  Step 3 — Add industry-specific processes on top of the standard ones

  Beyond the universal SAP process patterns, every industry has processes unique to it. For mining, these were:

  • Plan to Produce — mine production scheduling and aluminum output, which doesn't exist in a retail or financial services landscape
  • Freight and Logistics Management — aluminum concentrate shipments from mine site to smelter
  • Project and Capital Portfolio Management — capital project governance for mine expansions
  • ESG and Sustainability Reporting — GHG emissions, Aluminum Mark certification, water usage KPIs

These came directly from reading Client’s Annual Report and identifying what makes their business distinct from a generic enterprise.

The rule of thumb: 70% of your Business Contexts will come from standard process patterns. 30% will be industry-specific and will come from the client's own public documents, Annual Report, or strategy presentations.

  ---

  Step 4 — Identify cross-cutting processes last

 Some Business Contexts don't map neatly to a single Business Capability — they cut across multiple ones. In my LeanIX tenant:

  • Enterprise Architecture Management crosses IT Management, Application Development, and Sustainability BCs
  • Business Process Intelligence crosses multiple BCs via the Signavio toolchain

Leave these for last. Once you have all the obvious end-to-end processes mapped, the gaps that remain are usually the cross-cutting ones.

  ---

  Step 5 — Validate by linking, not by thinking

 The real test of whether a Business Context is correctly defined is whether you can link it coherently to:

  • At least one Business Capability (the what)
  • At least one Application (the how)
  • At least one Organisation (the who)

If you can't make all three links for a Business Context, either the context is too vague, or it's actually a sub-process that should be absorbed into a parent context rather than standing alone.

 Initiative Factsheet:

I wanted to give a clear picture of the Enterprise. So, I created various Initiative factsheets based on the Objective factsheets I had from the Business Strategy Map. To achieve those Objectives and based on their size, I divided the Initiatives into short-term, mid-term and long-term categories by putting them under Idea, Program, Project and EPIC sub-categories. I also created milestones in those Initiatives to have realistic dates put to it. This was one of the most time-consuming factsheets I worked on, along with the Business Context factsheet.

I populated the following fields after creating the factsheet:

  • Name
  • Subtype
  • Description
  • Lifecycle
  • Milestones

Once I had these fields populated, I linked it to the Applications, Business Capabilities, IT Components, Objectives, Interfaces and Data Objects.   

Data Object Factsheet:

After having the Initiatives and Application factsheets in place, it is logical to have Data Objects in place. So, based on that I created the Data Objects and populated the following fields:

  • Name
  • Description

I then linked the Application, Interface and Initiative Factsheets to the Data Objects.

Interface Factsheet:

Once I have all the Applications and Data Objects in place, it is time to identify the various interfaces in the landscape. I populated the following fields in the Interface factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description
  • Interface Providers
  • Interface Consumers
  • Data Flow Direction
  • Interface Type
  • Frequency

 Once these were in place, I linked the interface to Data Object, IT Component and Initiative Factsheets.

The most analytically valuable field on an Interface fact sheet is often overlooked: the IT Component link. This field identifies the middleware, the integration platform that physically moves data between the provider and consumer Applications. In my landscape, all interfaces between SAP SuccessFactors systems (HCM Core → ECP, HCM Core → WFA, ECP → WFA) use SAP Integration Suite as the middleware IT Component. Linking it here means that when SAP Integration Suite appears in the ITC Roadmap as retiring or changing, you immediately know which interfaces, and therefore which Application-to-Application data flows are at risk.
Always link the middleware ITC to your interfaces. It turns the Interface Circle Map from a connectivity diagram into a risk and impact analysis tool.

Contract Factsheet:

This is a new factsheet that has been recently introduced by LeanIX Product team. This factsheet is an optional factsheet and used to store information about contracts and the value the contract holds.

The client/customer sometimes wants to see which Business Capability is spending how much money using these Applications. So, I added this factsheet to showcase the cost of various business capabilities.  The Business Capability Cost Report showcases this in form of a horizontal bar chart.

I added the following fields and then linked them to Application factsheets in the ‘Attached Applications’ section of the Contract factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description
  • Contract Financial Information

Dashboards:

Once you have created all the factsheets above and linked them, it is now time to add Reports to Dashboard. Go to the default Dashboard, edit the Dashboard, add the Report panel from the right and select various Reports from the drop-down. After that, every time you log in, you’ll see those selected Reports.

Since I created all these factsheets based on MetaModel and linked them, the following Reports helped me create a clear holistic picture on the Dashboard:

  • Business Strategy Map
  • Business Capability Map
  • High-level Initiative Roadmap
  • Baseline Application Architecture
  • Target Application Architecture
  • Transformation Roadmap
  • Business Capabilities per Application
  • Business objectives mapped to Business Capabilities
  • APM Roadmap
  • Interface Circle Map
  • IT Component Roadmap
  • Lifecycle & Age
  • Business Capability Cost

A note on assumptions and the living model:

  Every EA model built from public information rather than direct client input will contain assumptions. This is not a weakness. It is a feature. A model with structured, documented assumptions is infinitely more useful to a Leadership team than a blank workspace waiting for perfect data.

  The assumptions I made follow a clear hierarchy:

  • Tier 1 (high confidence): Fact sheets derived directly from contracts, subscriptions, or public filings. Application names, IT Component versions, Objective statements from the Annual Report.
  • Tier 2 (medium confidence): Relationships and attributes inferred from industry knowledge. Business Capability linkages, Business Context process names, lifecycle dates aligned to known project timelines.
  • Tier 3 (low confidence, flagged for review): Organizational structure, maturity ratings, and cost allocations where no source document existed.

 The right way to use this model is as a conversation starter, not a final deliverable. Present it to the Leadership team and client Architects with the explicit invitation: "Here is what the data tells us, where does this match your reality, and where does it need to change?" That conversation, guided by a populated model, will produce better outcomes in one meeting than months of waiting for the client to provide perfect data upfront.

 The model is a living artifact. Update it as the conversation evolves.

entry.title: Product Cost Planning at a Reference Site in SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01
entry.content:
Simplifying Product Cost Planning for Retail and Fashion Organizations

Retail and fashion companies typically manage hundreds of stores, multiple distribution centers, and hundreds of thousands—or even millions—of articles. While the procurement and valuation processes across these locations are often identical, Product Cost Planning has traditionally been executed separately for every valuation area.
For organizations operating at this scale, this results in lengthy costing runs, increased database growth, unnecessary system processing, and additional maintenance effort.
To address this challenge, SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 introduces Product Cost Planning at a Reference Site, enabling organizations to calculate product costs once for a designated reference site and subsequently distribute the planned prices to operational sites.
This new capability significantly reduces processing effort while ensuring consistent product valuation across locations that share identical business characteristics.

Why Use a Reference Site for Product Cost Planning?
In many retail organizations, hundreds of DC's/stores purchase the same merchandise under identical procurement conditions.
Consider a typical scenario:
• Every DC/store purchases the same article (with different sizes) from the same vendor.
• Purchase prices are identical. usually at the generic article level.
• Inventory valuation follows the same costing logic.
Despite these similarities, traditional Product Cost Planning executes costing per variant article separately for every valuation area.
For an organization with 500 stores and 120,000 articles, this means generating approximately 60 million costing records, even though the calculated costs are often identical.
The result is longer batch runtimes, higher HANA resource consumption, and unnecessary duplication of data.
With SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01, organizations can now calculate product costs once at a Reference Site and reuse those costs across all eligible receiving sites.
Why Use a Reference Site for Product Cost Planning?
Reference Sites have long been an integral part of SAP Retail. Organizations use them to centralize common master data, reducing maintenance effort and ensuring consistency across operational sites.
Historically, Reference Sites have supported:
• Article master maintenance
• Listing
• Assortments
• Purchasing information
• Pricing
• Logistics master data
With SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01, SAP extends this proven concept to Product Cost Planning.
Rather than creating identical cost estimates for every receiving site, organizations can now:
1. Designate a Reference Site.
2. Perform Product Cost Planning only for that site.
3. Mark the resulting cost estimates.
4. Copy the future planned prices to assigned receiving sites.
5. Release the planned prices centrally.
The enhancement reduces redundant calculations while preserving consistent financial valuation.

How the New Solution Works
Instead of executing Product Cost Planning for every operational site, the calculation is performed once for the Reference Site. The resulting future prices are then distributed to all eligible receiving sites before being released as standard prices.
This reduces duplicated processing while maintaining a single, consistent standard cost across the landscape.

Prerequisites
A prerequisite for using the solution is that the Retail switch (ISR_RETAILSWITCH) is activated.
Using the new material price table, FMLT_PRICE, is optional. The solution functions effectively with or without it.

Step 1 – Assign the Reference Site

Transaction: REFSITE

Create the Reference Site and assign receiving plants.

Reference plant groupings can span multiple company codes, allowing centralized Product Cost Planning across organizational structures.

Step 2 – Edit the Selection List

Transaction: CKMATCON

Generate the material selection list used during Product Cost Planning. You can also implement BAdI BADI_RFM_PROD_COSTING_VARIANT to determine custom logic for identifying the reference material.

Step 3 – Generate the Reference Material Selection List

Transaction: S_ERX_29000720 - Create Selection List for Reference Site

Execute the report to generate a selection list containing only reference materials assigned to the Reference Site.

You can also implement BAdI BADI_RFM_PROD_COSTING_VARIANT to determine custom logic for identifying the reference material.

Step 4 – Execute the Costing Run

Transaction: CK40N

Create the costing run using the newly generated selection list.

Execute the costing process through the Mark step.

At this point, do not release the prices.

Proceed instead to copying future prices.

Step 5 – Copy Future Prices

Transaction: CK_COPY

This report distributes future planned prices from the Reference Site to all receiving plants across all active valuation views.

If company codes use different currencies, the system automatically performs currency conversion.

Two copy options are available:

  • Redistribute All – overwrite all existing planned prices.
  • Incremental Copy – copy only where planned prices do not already exist.
  • Prerequisites
    • Actual Cost Component Split settings must be consistent between reference and receiving plants.
    • Reference materials must be costed and marked for all active valuation views.

Step 6 – Release Future Prices

Transaction: CKME

Reports

  • SAPRCK23_JOB_SPLITTER
  • SAPRCKM_PRCH_JOB_SPLITTER

For large data volumes, SAP recommends using the wrapper reports to split processing into multiple packages, significantly improving release performance.

Example

Suppose an article has:

Cost Component

Value

Purchase Price

$12.00

Freight

$1.00

Handling

$0.50

Standard Cost

$13.50

Instead of calculating this cost estimate separately for every receiving distribution center:

  • DC1001 → $13.50
  • DC1002 → $13.50
  • DC1003 → $13.50
  • ...

SAP performs the calculation once:

Reference Site → $13.50

The planned price is then copied automatically to all eligible receiving sites.

Expected Performance Improvements

The primary objective of this enhancement is not to change the costing logic—it is to eliminate redundant calculations.

Typical improvements observed include:

Metric

Traditional

Reference Site

CK40N Runtime

High

Significantly Reduced

Cost Estimate Volume

Very High

Reduced

HANA Memory Usage

Higher

Lower

The exact improvement depends on:

  • Number of receiving Sites
  • Number of Articles
  • Procurement model
  • System landscape
  • Parallel processing configuration
Considerations Before Adoption
The underlying principle of this solution is simple: if the business conditions are the same, the cost estimate should also be the same.
Consider a retailer selling a T-shirt available in multiple colors but identical in size, procurement source, and purchasing conditions. Whether the T-shirt is gray or white, the calculated standard cost remains unchanged. Likewise, if all sites procure the article under the same commercial and valuation conditions, there is no business justification for generating separate cost estimates for every variant at every store.

Instead, SAP calculates the cost estimate once at the Reference Site and reuses it across all eligible receiving sites, eliminating redundant calculations while ensuring consistent product valuation.

 
Implementation guidelines
  • Group together sites with identical procurement characteristics.
  • Validate purchasing conditions before every costing cycle.
  • Schedule CK40N as a background job.
  • Regularly review cost component splits.
  • Monitor costing logs and exceptions.
Document the business rationale for using shared costing to support governance and audit requirements.

entry.title: Re: /BIC/FFIAR_C03M table partition nearing 2billion reco...
entry.content:
For the issue that table is reaching to 2 billion limited, and this can be handled from HANA DB side.

You could review this note below and perform a reorg for the large table as mentioned in note:

2019973 - Handling Very Large Data Volumes in SAP BW on SAP HANA
2374652 - Handling Very Large Data Volumes in SAP BW/4HANA

-> Handling Large Data Volumes - 2 Billion Limit Value Reached

As a remind that you need apply file TABLE_PLACEMENT.zip before the reorg as mentioned in this note.

entry.title: Manage Sales Documents App: Introducing Implicit Filters
entry.content:
Background

In this blog, I'm going to write about some implicit filters in the app Manage Sales Documents (MSD).

The Filters

Filter Data via Custom Fields

This is a long-standing impediment in VA05. Now it is tackled partially in MSD.

I assume that there are already custom fields in your systems, and you have been using them in the various sales documents. The expectation is to be able to use them as filters to query data in the list report.

The first thing to do is to Enable the fields for the service of MSD in Custom Fields app.

There are two fields in this example: one for the header, the other for the item.

Custom Field for Sales Document Header

Preparation

Create a field for header:

Enable usage for the service of MSD

Custom Field for Sales Document Item

Preparation

Create a field for item

Enable usage for the service of MSD

Consumption in MSD

Use adapt filters to expose the two filter fields.

The header filter works on all three tabs.

The item filter works on items & schedule lines tabs.

Since it has no effect on Sales Documents tab, as a result, this tab loads all data, an information will be displayed to explain the behavior: Filter "Extended Item Date" is not relevant for tab "Sales Documents". Setting this filter has no effect on the results.'

Sales Document (One-Time Customer) Filter

When using a one-time customer to create sales orders, it is mandatory to change the contact info such as address, name, phone number, email etc. However, the customer number stays the same, thus, it is not possible to precisely filter the documents using just the number. So, we built this filter that can query sales documents via the contact info of a one-time customer.

Open the value help

Filter data via Telephone

Filter data via Name

entry.title: How to show a comparison of price lists or last prices with vendor names from a Purchase Order
entry.content:
Hello,

I have a specific business requirement in SAP Business One. When a user is creating a Purchase Order (PO) and fills in the Item No., they want to see a quick comparison of prices for that item from various suppliers directly from the document row.

Ideally, they want to be able to right-click on the 'Unit Price' column and see an option like "Show Price List" (or similar) which pops up a window showing:

Vendor/Supplier Names

The prices they offer (either from multiple Price Lists or from Last Purchase Prices history).

For now, our standard approach that we already setup uses 'Unit Price' click on the right side mouse column to open "Last Prices", but I prefer to trigger it directly from the 'Unit Price' too but when click on the right side mouse it will show 'Price List' option.

Is there a standard way to achieve this? If not, what is the best customization approach?

Thank you in advance for your insights!

Best Regards,
Ester

entry.title: Re: Project HomeControl 2 · Part 2 — A UI5 frontend, built from non-UI5 Web Components
entry.content:
Thanks Nico — this is exactly the pointer the post needed, and your write-up is the reference I should have linked.

You're right: sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent already does generically what I built by hand — the tag rendering, the property-vs-attribute mutator, forwarding object properties onto the element without a full re-render, slots and events. I went through the source after your comment and it lines up one-to-one with the "pitfalls" I described. So for anyone with a fixed set of components, extending the base class per element is the idiomatic route, and it's a lot less code than my wrapper.

My choice to roll it by hand was deliberate, for two reasons — and I'll make that clearer in the post. First, teaching: writing the glue myself was the only way to show why .data has to be a property and not an attribute, and where the upgrade-order and re-render traps sit. It's easier to appreciate what the base class hands you once you've done it the hard way. Second, the shape: I wanted one generic control that wraps any tag and takes a whole object as a single data property, rather than a typed control per element — handy for a bespoke, fast-moving design system where I'm not maintaining metadata per component. Different trade-off, not a better one.

I'll add a callout near "the bridge" that names the base class and links your article, and frames the hand-rolled version as the under-the-hood explanation rather than the recommended path. Really appreciate you taking the time — this is the kind of comment that makes the Community worth writing for.

entry.title: KKF1报错结算规则无法被创建
entry.content: CO 生产订单KKF1创建时提示结算规则无法创建。我们之前有好几个CO订单类型，我只是新增了订单类型用于新工厂业务。查看了KOT2已经分配了结算规则 具体报错信息如下： 结算规则没能被创建 消息编号 KO311 诊断 只有在把 结算规则 分配到 CO 生产订单（无数量结构的生产订单）后才可以结算该订单发生的实际成本。 在创建期间，系统自动把结算规则分配到该订单。 在此情况下，无法为该订单生成任何结算规则，因为控制该结算规则创建的缺省值尚未存储在系统中。 在您创建该结算规则时系统发出了此错误消息： [KD021]缺省规则  不存在

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud Seamless Planning入門 - Part2 : Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう
entry.content:
このブログシリーズは、SAP Business Data CloudのSeamless Planningの全体像を一通り把握していただくことを目的としています。

このシリーズは、以下で構成されます。
- Part1 : Seamless Planningとは
- Part2 : Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう（本ブログ）
- Part3 : Seamless PlanningでSAP Datasphereの実績ビューをライブバージョンで参照しよう
- to be continued ...

本ブログ「Part2 : Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう」では、実際にSeamless Planningモデルを作成する手順と、作成後にSAP Datasphere側でデータを確認する方法を解説します。
 

目次

1. Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成
2. ファクトテーブルをSAP Datasphereに公開
3. SAP Datasphereでデータを確認

1. Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成

SAP Analytics Cloudの計画モデルはデータ保存先を「SAP Analytics Cloud」もしくは「SAP Datasphere」に選択することができます。SAP Datasphereを選択することでSeamless Planningの機能をご利用いただくことができます。

手順：

1.1. SAP Analytics Cloudのナビゲーションバーから 「モデラ」＞新規作成「モデル」＞ 「次へ」を選択します。（今回は「空のモデルから開始」の場合をご案内します）

 1.2. データ保管場所として「SAP Datasphere」を選択し、使用したいスペースを選択して「作成」をクリックします。

1.3.  以降はメジャーやディメンションの設定を行い、モデルを保存します。

これでまずはSAP Datasphereにデータが保管されるSAP Analytics Cloudの計画モデルが作成できました。

 

2. ファクトテーブルをSAP Datasphereに公開

 

SAP Analytics Cloud上で計画モデルを作成・保存しただけでは、SAP DatasphereのData Builder上からファクトテーブルを参照することができません。SAP Analytics Cloud側から「公開」操作を行う必要があります。この操作によって、SAP DatasphereのモデラがSAP Analytics Cloudの計画データをビューや分析モデルのソースとして利用できるようになります。

手順：

2.1. SAP Analytics Cloudで1で作成した計画モデルを開き、右側のモデル詳細パネルにて「SAP Datasphereでの公開」＞「編集...」をクリックします。

 
2.2. 「SAP Datasphereでデータファンデーショnをファクトテーブルとして...1」にチェックが入ってることを確認し、ファクトテーブルの名前を入力して「OK」をクリックします 。

2.3. モデルを保存します。

 これでSAP Analytics Cloudの計画データをSAP Datasphereでも使用できるようになりました。

3. SAP Datasphereでデータを確認

SAP Datasphere側にファクトテーブルが格納されているかを確認してみましょう。

手順：

3.1. SAP Analytics Cloudの右上「製品切り替え」よりSAP Datasphereに切り替えます。

 3.2. ナビゲーションバーの「データビルダ」より対象のスペースを選択すると、2で公開したファクトテーブルを確認することができました。

SAP Datasphereにて、SAP Analytics Cloudの計画データをビューや分析モデルのソースとして利用できるようになります。 

 

以上、Seamless Planningモデルの作成とSAP Datasphereでのデータ確認方法を解説しました。次回のブログでは、SAP Datasphereのビューをライブバージョンとして計画モデルに接続し、実績データをリアルタイム参照する方法を解説します。

参考資料・ブログ

- Expose Objects for Consumption in SAP Datasphere | SAP Help Portal
- Seamless Planning with Datasphere & SAC: Seamless Planning in the Cloud

entry.title: Re: Request for information for SAP Intgeration Suite
entry.content:
Hi @c_walid 

What is the maximum number of concurrent iFlow executions (worker threads): This is depends on memory not iFlow. If I remember correct. Depends on subscription the nodes will be activated. Default it is 4 node can run in parallel from server side. 

When multiple instances of the same iFlow run in parallel, are they queued, rejected, or executed with degraded performance? What is the exact queuing/throttling behavior?: This will be queued based on the memory usage. 

Does concurrent execution of the same iFlow share any runtime resources (JVM heap, thread pool, Camel routes) that could cause performance degradation as concurrency increases?: Yes, it cause the performance issue. 

What is the maximum synchronous HTTP request processing timeout enforced by the Integration Suite runtime (Cloud Foundry router + CPI runtime) for a single message exchange? Is this configurable at the iFlow, tenant, or platform level?: Upto 9999999 milli seconds. Double check it please.

Is there a difference in timeout behavior between the HTTPS Sender adapter and other trigger types (e.g., timer-based, JMS-based)?: HTTPS is for rest ful connection. Timebased is for schedule the iFlow and run as a job. JMS is to Queue the message and re-process. 

If a message exceeds the timeout mid-processing, is there a way to receive a partial-comp: If you use multi-caste in the iFlow, yes it is possible. 

Regards,

Karthik Arjun
